text: The Amazon rainforest covers over 5.5 million square kilometers and is home to 10% of all species on Earth. It produces 20% of the world's oxygen and absorbs vast amounts of CO2.
The Great Wall of China stretches more than 21,000 kilometers across northern China. It was built over many centuries to protect Chinese states from invasions.
Mount Everest is the highest mountain in the world at 8,849 meters above sea level. It is located in the Himalayas on the border between Nepal and Tibet.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest ocean on Earth, covering more than 165 million square kilometers. It contains more than half of the world's oceanic water.
Albert Einstein developed the theory of relativity, which fundamentally changed our understanding of space, time, and gravity. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921.
The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the world, spanning across 11 countries in North Africa. It covers an area of about 9.2 million square kilometers.
DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, carries the genetic information for the development and functioning of all living organisms. Its double-helix structure was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953.
The Roman Empire at its height controlled vast territories spanning from Britain in the northwest to Mesopotamia in the east. It had a profound influence on Western civilization.
Shakespeare wrote 37 plays and 154 sonnets during his lifetime. He is widely regarded as the greatest writer in the English language.
The Internet was originally developed as ARPANET in the late 1960s by the US Department of Defense. Today it connects billions of devices worldwide.
The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others via synapses. It processes information using both electrical and chemical signals.
Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose and oxygen. It is the foundation of most food chains on Earth.
The Apollo 11 mission landed the first humans on the Moon on July 20, 1969. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the lunar surface while Michael Collins orbited above.
Antibiotics are medicines that fight infections caused by bacteria. Alexander Fleming discovered the first antibiotic, penicillin, in 1928 by observing mold killing bacteria on a petri dish.
The stock market crash of 1929 triggered the Great Depression, the most severe global economic downturn of the 20th century. Millions lost jobs and savings across the world.
Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns. Since the industrial revolution, human activities have been the main driver of climate change.
The periodic table organizes all known chemical elements by their atomic number and properties. It was first published by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.
The French Revolution began in 1789 and fundamentally transformed French society by dismantling the monarchy and aristocracy. It led to the rise of Napoleon Bonaparte.
Electricity is the flow of electric charge, typically carried by electrons moving through a conductor. Benjamin Franklin famously demonstrated its connection to lightning with a kite experiment.
The Black Death was a devastating pandemic that swept through Europe and Asia in the 14th century, killing an estimated 30-60% of Europe's population. It was caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis.
